checks.a_analysis: I examined the output text against the source PDF. The headings ('NC Parcels Project Status by County', 'Parcel Updates:', 'Most recent Quarter', 'Sources:'), the date ('January 8, 2025'), the list of legend items, the source description, and the scale bar text all have a direct basis in the source document. The paragraph containing the list of county abbreviations is an accurate extraction of the labels found on the map. No fabricated body text or invented sections were found.
The source document does not contain any phone numbers, email addresses, mailing addresses, or URLs. The output also contains no such information. Therefore, no fabrication occurred.

The source document is a single-page map. All textual components of the map, including the title, date, legend, county labels, source metadata, and scale bar, are present in the output. The graphical map is represented by an image element with descriptive alt text.
The source document does not contain any tables. This check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I compared the text content of the source and the output. All primary information (project status legend, county labels, and metadata) is preserved. While the 'NCGICC' logo text is not listed in the body text, it is appropriately included in the image alt text, which is an expected behavior for accessibility-focused conversions. There are no significant omissions of text content.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The output preserves the meaning and information of the source. Minor formatting differences, such as combining the scale bar numbers and unit ('0 25 50 100 Miles'), are acceptable reformatting. There are minor OCR artifacts in the county list (e.g., 'ALAMORAN' instead of 'ALAM' and 'ORAN'), but these are within the scope of acceptable OCR differences for a scanned document and do not fundamentally alter the information provided to a government employee.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed the source document and the JSON output. All text elements, including the title, date, legend items, and source metadata, appear exactly once in the output, consistent with their occurrence in the source. The list of county names extracted via OCR is included once as a paragraph element following the map image.

The heading hierarchy is correctly established. The document title is at the section level (H1), followed by 'Parcel Updates:' at level 2, 'Most recent Quarter' at level 3, and 'Sources:' at level 2. This represents a logical nested structure reflecting the visual layout of the legend and footer.
The source document does not contain any tables. The legend, while aligned, is functionally a list of categories which the pipeline has correctly represented as a list structure.

The reading order in the output follows the logical top-to-bottom flow of the document: Title -> Date -> Map Image -> County labels -> Legend -> Sources -> Scale. The placement of the OCR-extracted labels immediately after the image they belong to is an acceptable and logical ordering.
The legend in the source is a vertical list of parcel update statuses. The output captures this accurately as an unordered list, preserving the textual content and the categorical grouping.
There are no hyperlinks in the source document, and none have been erroneously added to the output.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output fundamentally changes the layout of the document. The source is a status map where information is organized spatially. The HTML conversion removes the map and converts the spatial labels into a dense, unformatted block of text. This removes the core visual organization and relationship between the county labels and the data they represent. Additionally, the legend has lost its color-coded organization, rendering it a simple text list.
checks.b_evidence_source: Page 1 displays a color-coded map of North Carolina counties with status labels (e.g., HYDE, DARE) placed within their geographic boundaries.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output presents the county labels as a single wall of text: 'HYDE DARE CART BEAU COLU ROBE BLAD SAMP HALI PEND BRUN...' with no geographic context or layout structure.

checks.a_analysis: The source document contains no tables; it is primarily a map with a legend and textual sources.

Several meaningful graphical elements are missing from the HTML output. Most importantly, the map of North Carolina—which is the primary content of the document—is completely absent. Additionally, the color swatches in the 'Parcel Updates' legend, which are essential for interpreting the data, are missing. The compass rose from the top left is also missing. While parts of the NCGICC logo are present, they are rendered at an incorrect scale and separated from their text components.
checks.b_evidence_source: The source PDF features a color-coded map of North Carolina, a compass rose, and colored squares in the 'Parcel Updates' legend.
checks.b_evidence_output: The HTML output is missing the map image, the compass rose, and the color swatches in the legend list.

checks.a_analysis: There is no evidence of body content duplication. Section headings appearing in both the table of contents and the main body is standard accessible design and does not constitute a defect.
